channel.title: NYT > Most Recent Headlines
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Los hongos subterráneos que sostienen la vida en la tierra
item.description: Mediante aprendizaje automático y un robot de imágenes de alta resolución, los científicos lograron medir y trazar el mapa de las enormes redes de hongos subterráneos que almacenan carbono.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes

item.title: ‘Hacks’ y la defensa de las amistades con diferencia de edad
item.description: En una época en la que las relaciones sociales se han debilitado, según los expertos, entablar amistad con alguien mayor o menor ofrece ventajas únicas.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Pearson

item.title: Los estadounidenses resienten cada vez más la brecha entre los ultrarricos y la gente promedio
item.description: Mientras Elon Musk se convierte en el primer “billonario” del mundo, los trabajadores se enfrentan a precios más altos y al temor a perder sus puestos de trabajo por culpa de la IA.
item.dc:creator: Ben Casselman

media:content.media:credit: Karsten Moran para The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Times Square el viernes, cuando SpaceX, de Elon Musk, salió a bolsa en el Nasdaq.

item.title: Tweet, Delete, Repeat: Social Media Posts Overshadow N.Y. House Race
item.description: Darializa Avila Chevalier won the backing of Mayor Zohran Mamdani in her bid to unseat Representative Adriano Espaillat. Then her social media history took center stage.
item.dc:creator: Sally Goldenberg and Maya King

media:content.media:credit: Nicole Craine for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Darializa Avila Chevalier is running in the Democratic primary in New York’s 13th Congressional District in Upper Manhattan and parts of the Bronx.

item.title: The Last Days of the Times Square Red Lobster
item.description: Regulars and newcomers feasted on seafood and biscuits as the restaurant’s bright red lobster logo glowed in Times Square for the final time.
item.dc:creator: Claire Fahy

media:content.media:credit: Gabby Jones for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Some diners at the Red Lobster in Times Square ate their seafood on Friday not knowing that the restaurant was about to close for good. But this group knew, and went all out.

item.title: ‘He Offered to Take Me to Get the Best Hamburger in the City’
item.description: Catching a glimpse of an elusive bird, the sax player’s tune and more reader tales of New York City in this week’s Metropolitan Diary.

item.title: NYT Spelling Bee Answers for June 14, 2026
item.description: Feeling stuck on today’s puzzle? We can help.
item.dc:creator: New York Times Games

item.title: ‘A Lifetime Dream’: Knicks Fans Celebrate From New York City to Texas
item.description: Dancing in the East Village. Flooding San Antonio with Knicks’ orange and blue. Crying at Madison Square Garden. The first N.B.A. championship in decades had fans in a frenzy.
item.dc:creator: Santul Nerkar

media:content.media:credit: Pool photo by Eric Gay
media:content.media:description: The jersey of the Knicks’ All-Star point guard Jalen Brunson could be seen on children and adults all across New York City during the team’s championship run. He was named the most valuable player of the finals.

item.title: President Trump Endorses Mike Collins in Georgia Senate Runoff
item.description: Mr. Trump backed Mr. Collins over Derek Dooley, a former football coach who is supported by Gov. Brian Kemp, a Republican whose relationship with the president is strained.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk

media:content.media:credit: Audra Melton for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Representative Mike Collins, Republican of Georgia, received President Trump’s coveted endorsement on Sunday.

item.title: New Yorkers Waited 53 Years for This. Time to Celebrate.
item.description: With a championship run for the ages, the Knicks awakened joy in a city desperate for a winner.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times and Caitlyn Freeman

media:content.media:credit: Shuran Huang for The New York Times

item.title: Knicks Will Get a Ticker-Tape Parade on Thursday
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani will present the team with the keys to the city. He said the parade will be the Knicks’ first in team history.
item.dc:creator: Andy Newman

media:content.media:credit: Adrienne Grunwald
media:content.media:description: Crowds in Brooklyn celebrated the Knicks’ Game 5 win on Saturday.

item.title: Iran War Live Updates: U.S. and Tehran Send Mixed Signals on Emerging Peace Agreement
item.description: President Trump said it would be signed on Sunday, but Iran’s Foreign Ministry cautioned that the timeline could be slower.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

media:content.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times

item.title: Taylor Swift entra en el Salón de la Fama de los Compositores con lágrimas, bromas y una advertencia
item.description: Swift es la mujer más joven en recibir este reconocimiento. Entre los nuevos miembros de este año también se encontraban Gene Simmons y Paul Stanley de Kiss, y Alanis Morissette.
item.dc:creator: Malia Mendez

media:content.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: In a speech, Taylor Swift called songwriting “pretty much the only thing I ever just naturally did.”

item.title: Bob Dylan and Liza Minnelli Already Turned 80. They Have Thoughts for Trump.
item.description: “I feel the same as I did 50 years ago,” President Trump said. “It’s crazy.”
item.dc:creator: New York Times Opinion

item.title: Japan Is Running Out of Royals. Are More Men the Answer?
item.description: Japan’s legislature is drafting a plan to allow the imperial family to adopt distant male relatives. But some in Japan would prefer a female emperor.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández and Hisako Ueno

media:content.media:credit: Pool photo by Kazuhiro Nogi
media:content.media:description: Emperor Naruhito of Japan, center, with Empress Masako and other royal family members at a garden party in April in Tokyo.

item.title: Germany and Japan Are Rearming Again, 80 Years After World War II
item.description: After becoming allies to disastrous effect in the 1940s, Berlin and Tokyo are finding new reasons to team up — including rebuilding their militaries.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley and Javier C. Hernández
item.category: Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
United States Defense and Military Forces

media:content.media:credit: Pool photo by David Mareuil
media:content.media:description: Shinjiro Koizumi, Japan’s defense minister, and his German counterpart, Boris Pistorius, at a naval base in Yokosuka, Japan, in March. The countries have been building up their militaries.

item.title: In Venezuela, a 91-Year-Old Weaver Sticks to Traditions
item.description: Though electric machines are now standard, the Venezuelan weaver Margarita Mora has clung to a mix of ancestral Indigenous and Spanish practices to create surprisingly modern work.
item.dc:creator: Silvia Benedetti and Andrea Hernández

media:content.media:credit: Andrea Hernandez for The New York Times

item.title: Listen to This Sondheim Masterpiece and It Will Bring Order to Your Day
item.description: Stephen Sondheim’s masterpiece, “Sunday in the Park With George,” contains a song capable of making order out of mayhem. Listen to hear how he did it.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.category: Sunday in the Park with George (Play)

item.title: Quote of the Day: World Cup Peels Argentines Away From Phones and Has Them Stuck on Stickers
item.description: Quotation of the Day for Sunday, June 14, 2026.

item.title: Voters Reject Anti-Islam Candidate in Mayoral Race in Dallas Suburb
item.description: The election for mayor of Frisco, Texas, became a referendum on diversity and a test of anti-Muslim messaging in a general election contest.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman

media:content.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Mark Hill, a lawyer and civic booster, won a sharply contested race for mayor of Frisco, Texas

item.title: Trump Administration Reignites Its Feud With Anthropic Over Latest A.I. Models
item.description: The administration’s surprise restrictions Friday cut off foreign access to Anthropic’s latest models and sparked another round of finger pointing.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz, Julian E. Barnes and Ana Swanson
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

media:content.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: An Anthropic event in San Francisco, last month.

item.title: State Attorneys General Are Investigating OpenAI
item.description: OpenAI said that a coalition of states had opened an investigation over a wide range of its practices, including its handling of user data, safety of minors and advertising activities.
item.dc:creator: Cecilia Kang and David McCabe

media:content.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
media:content.media:description: “We take the concerns raised by state attorneys general seriously,” OpenAI, headed by Sam Altman, said in a statement on Saturday.

item.title: Trump Again Picks Personal Lawyer for a Top Job, as U.S. Attorney in Manhattan
item.description: James M. McDonald, a veteran former federal prosecutor and regulator, has more recently been part of President Trump’s legal team, appealing his criminal conviction.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman, Benjamin Weiser and Alan Feuer
item.category: New York State Criminal Case Against Trump (71543-23)

media:content.media:credit: John Taggart for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: James M. McDonald is a litigation partner at Sullivan & Cromwell, the law firm handling President Trump’s appeal of his criminal conviction in a Manhattan state court.

item.title: Fans Swarm New York to Celebrate Knicks’ N.B.A. Title
item.description: Knicks fans shut down parts of New York City after their team defeated the San Antonio Spurs to win the N.B.A. championship for the first time since 1973.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

media:content.media:credit: Shuran Huang for The New York Times

item.title: Top Haitian Security Official Kidnapped
item.description: A security expert who had recently become chief of staff to the new defense minister was abducted, the latest example of violence gripping the country.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles

media:content.media:credit: Odelyn Joseph/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Cars burned by armed gangs in downtown Port-au-Prince, Haiti, in January.

item.title: Yosemite Ranger’s Suit Over Trans Flag at El Capitan Is Dismissed
item.description: The ranger, who was fired after hanging the flag, sued the government on free speech grounds. On Friday, a judge dismissed the case, saying the court lacked the authority to rule.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald

media:content.media:credit: Tracy Barbutes/Reuters
media:content.media:description: Shannon Joslin, a quantitative wildlife biologist with the National Park Service, helped to hang a transgender pride flag at Yosemite National Park in May 2025. Dr. Joslin was fired that August.

item.title: Limited Runs
item.description: John Kugelman says a lot with a little.
item.dc:creator: Caitlin Lovinger

media:content.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: “Wha?” or “Wah!”

item.title: Mystery Potato Hovering Over Colorado Is Reported in Latest U.F.O. Files
item.description: Potatoes have featured in sightings before, and in real and fictional space travel.
item.dc:creator: Danny Hakim
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: 5 Children Are Killed After Van and S.U.V. Collide in Rural Ontario
item.description: The children — four girls and one boy — were among 10 people in the van at the time of the crash. An infant was seriously injured, the police said.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker

item.title: At the Kennedy Center, a Name Change Shrouded in Uncertainty
item.description: President Trump’s name was removed from the arts institution’s facade overnight on Saturday. Many questions remain, including whether or not it stays off.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Williamson and Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts

media:content.media:credit: Rahmat Gul/Ap Photo/Rahmat Gul
media:content.media:description: The Kennedy Center certified on Saturday that President Trump’s name had been removed from the building, but did not give a clear answer on when the tarps would be removed.

item.title: Threats, Free Speech and the Case Against U. of Michigan Activists
item.description: Prosecutors accused eight pro-Palestinian activists with ties to the university of conspiring to threaten campus leaders and others.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith

media:content.media:credit: Nic Antaya for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The University of Michigan campus in Ann Arbor.

item.title: Trump’s Name Is Removed From Kennedy Center Facade
item.description: Workers removed President Trump’s name from the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts on Saturday following a judge’s order.
item.dc:creator: Cynthia Silva
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts

media:content.media:credit: Pete Kiehart for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: On Friday night, workers constructed scaffolding near President Trump’s name on the facade of the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts.

item.title: ‘Witness’ Tree Outside Abraham Lincoln’s Former Home Irreparably Damaged
item.description: Storms damaged the American linden tree, which was alive when Lincoln lived at the home in Springfield, Ill. He left it in 1861 for the White House.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner

media:content.media:credit: Seth Perlman/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Abraham Lincoln’s home in Springfield, Ill. On Thursday, severe storms irreparably damaged a “witness” tree that dated back to Lincoln’s time there.

item.title: Dito van Reigersberg, Avant-Garde and Drag Virtuoso, Dies at 53
item.description: A co-founder of Pig Iron Theater Company, known for its surreal productions, he also gave energetic performances as his alter ego, Martha Graham Cracker.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw

item.title: James Talarico Plays Up Spurs’ Past Glory in Campaign Ad Aimed at Texas Voters
item.description: The Democratic candidate for Senate looked to gain crossover appeal with a spot emphasizing teamwork and his own experience as a teacher in San Antonio.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk

media:content.media:credit: Antranik Tavitian for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: James Talarico, the Democratic Senate candidate in Texas, is trying to improve his standing with male voters.

item.title: The Young Streamers Who Are Trying to ‘Make It Big’ Off Knicks Mania
item.description: They’re flooding Midtown Manhattan to make content. They’re irritating some lifelong fans. But they see a one-of-a-kind opportunity to blow up online
item.dc:creator: Troy Closson and Miles G. Cohen

media:content.media:credit: Vincent Alban for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Streamers captured the mania that followed the Knicks’ epic comeback on Wednesday in Game 4 of the N.B.A. finals.

item.title: El gobierno de Trump dice que reanudará el procesamiento de solicitudes de asilo e inmigración
item.description: La respuesta se produjo después de que un juez federal reprendiera a los funcionarios por no cumplir de inmediato una orden dictada la semana pasada.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo and Zach Montague

media:content.media:credit: Madison Swart para The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Un tribunal de inmigración en Nueva York en mayo. Más de un millón de solicitudes de asilo y migración se habían paralizado debido a las políticas del gobierno de Trump.

item.title: Vandals Damage Work by Black Artist at Houston Museum, Officials Say
item.description: Two men scraped and punctured a $23,000 painting at the Houston Museum of African American Culture last month, the museum said. It decided to display the damaged artwork.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.category: Houston Museum of African American Culture

media:content.media:credit: Houston Museum of African American Culture
media:content.media:description: The damaged painting, “Man in the Garden” by the artist Clarence Heyward, was part of an exhibition called “Eden.”

item.title: EE. UU. y Venezuela afirman haber abatido a un líder del Tren de Aragua en una operación conjunta
item.description: Un ataque realizado esta semana en Venezuela acabó con la vida de un líder de la banda conocido como Niño Guerrero, quien era buscado en Estados Unidos, dijeron autoridades de ambos países.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

media:content.media:credit: Eric Lee para The New York Times
media:content.media:description: El presidente Trump dijo que la operación se había llevado a cabo en estrecha colaboración con el nuevo Gobierno venezolano.

item.title: WABC Anchor Bill Ritter Is Stepping Down After Alzheimer’s Diagnosis
item.description: The veteran journalist told viewers on Friday that it would be his last time anchoring the newscast, but that he was assuming a new role covering the disease he’s fighting.
item.dc:creator: Aimee Ortiz

media:content.media:credit: Craig Barritt/Getty Images for Hearst
media:content.media:description: Bill Ritter, who has anchored the 6 p.m. broadcast of WABC’s “Eyewitness News” since 2001, is stepping down.

item.title: What U.S. and Paraguay Fans Have in Common
item.description: At the first U.S. home World Cup match against Paraguay, the colors and the chants were loud in Los Angeles.
item.dc:creator: Simbarashe Cha

item.title: The Knicks Are the Hottest Ticket in Town. Hottest Jersey, Too.
item.description: Unless you’re a size 2X or 3X, you might be out of luck when you go looking for official N.B.A. merchandise in New York City.
item.dc:creator: Kaja Andric

media:content.media:credit: Reece Williams for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Fans waiting outside the N.B.A. store on Fifth Avenue on Friday.

item.title: David Plowden, Who Photographed a Disappearing America, Dies at 93
item.description: With his haunting images of steam locomotives, steel mills and Midwestern farms, the celebrated lensman revealed the poetry in the artifacts of manual labor.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams

item.title: How David Hockney Taught Los Angeles to See Itself
item.description: A Brit, he became a symbol of the city’s culture, stylish and alienating, with his vivid swimming pool paintings and embrace of SoCal light, hedonism and gay liberation.
item.dc:creator: Jori Finkel

media:content.media:credit: Nathanael Turner for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: David Hockney repainted his own pool — and signed and dated it July 8, 2017, the day before his 80th birthday.

item.title: Only One President Could Come Up With This Spectacle
item.description: Trump has gone beyond kayfabe.
item.dc:creator: Jamelle Bouie

media:content.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times

item.title: A Dinner for People Who Like to Press on Things
item.description: Smashed chicken with corn and cabbage is super satisfying, both to make and to eat.
item.dc:creator: Mia Leimkuhler

media:content.media:credit: Christopher Testani for The New York Times. Food Stylist: Simon Andrews.
media:content.media:description: Ashley Lonsdale’s smashed chicken with corn and cabbage.

item.title: It Was 1973, and the Knicks Were Heading to Game 5 …
item.description: Lifelong fans reminisced on the team’s last championship win. “When the Knicks won, it was like a different New York City,” one said.
item.dc:creator: Gabriella Gershenson and Jesse McKinley

media:content.media:credit: Getty Images
media:content.media:description: Dave DeBusschere in the 1973 N.B.A. Finals. The Knicks forward’s tenacious rebounding helped propel the franchise to its only two championships.

item.title: Ben Mims Loves an Erewhon Brownie
item.description: A celebrated baker and cookbook author takes the Bake Time Questionnaire.
item.dc:creator: Vaughn Vreeland

media:content.media:credit: David Malosh for The New York Times. Food Stylist: Simon Andrews.
media:content.media:description: Melissa Clark’s easy buttermilk peach cobbler.

item.title: Jon Ossoff’s Anti-Authoritarian Playbook
item.description: The Opinion columnist Michelle Goldberg returned from a recent trip to Hungary with an appreciation for Senator Jon Ossoff’s playbook. Viktor Orban’s stunning defeat demonstrates the power of an anticorruption message, she argues, and Ossoff offers a model for Democrats.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Goldberg and Ingrid Holmquist
